serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged bottle or can of water chestnut sugar cane drink is approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing about 67.5 kcal and 11.25 g of carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Water Chestnut Sugar Cane Drink (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Water Chestnut Sugar Cane Drink (Less Sugar, Packaged), cr_id D05010040. All per-100g values are as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Sugar cane juice and analogous sweetened beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for packaged water chestnut sugar cane drink (less sugar). GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sugar cane juice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 43–70 for cane juice and lightly sweetened beverages). The 'less sugar' formulation (4.4 g sugar/100 g) places this at the lower end of the medium range. GL per 250 g serving = (65 × 11.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar in a packaged sugar-sweetened beverage; sodium is low at 2 mg/100 g but SSB classification applies
hypertension.guidance: Despite very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), this packaged drink contains added sugar (4.4 g/100 g) and is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage; AHA guidelines recommend limiting SSBs for hypertension management, and individuals should choose plain water or genuinely unsweetened beverages instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar and zero dietary fibre; liquid carbohydrates raise postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage contains 4.4 g added sugar per 100 g with zero fibre, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid all SSBs including 'less sugar' variants and choose plain water or unsweetened drinks.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular SSB consumption is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk; zero saturated fat and very low sodium are positive factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While this drink is fat-free and very low in sodium (2 mg/100 g), its classification as a sugar-sweetened beverage means regular consumption is associated with increased triglycerides and cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines; occasional consumption is lower risk but plain water is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This sweetened beverage provides no omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, antioxidants, or dietary fibre, and its added sugar content promotes inflammatory mediators; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid SSBs and choose plain water or anti-inflammatory beverages such as green tea.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Water Chestnut Sugar Cane Drink (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink packaged beverage popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia, made from a blend of water chestnut extract and sugar cane juice with reduced added sugar. Water chestnuts (Eleocharis dulcis) are aquatic corm vegetables with a mild, slightly sweet, crisp flavour, while sugar cane juice contributes natural sweetness and a light grassy note. The 'less sugar' formulation contains 4.4 g of sugar per 100 g, significantly lower than standard versions, and provides only 27 kcal per 100 g, making it a low-calorie beverage. The drink is plant-based, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. Nutritionally, it is essentially a carbohydrate-only beverage with negligible protein and fat; it contains no dietary fibre and very low sodium (2 mg/100 g). Despite the 'less sugar' label, it remains classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) because added sugar is present. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension due to SSB classification, and provides no anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium) based on analogous sugar cane juice data. It is commonly sold in 250 ml bottles or cans and consumed as a refreshing snack drink, particularly in hawker centres and convenience stores across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010040, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All per-100g values are as reported by HPB. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database due to absence of a direct measurement for this specific product.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a carbohydrate-based beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients at detectable levels. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — the product contains no fat-contributing ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. saturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — the beverage is a filtered liquid with no intact fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D05010040 as of 2026. The input record included 'keto' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as the beverage contains 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and is not consistent with ketogenic dietary criteria. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sugar cane juice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific packaged product was found in approved sources.
